cards.back: Designated Router — reduces OSPF LSA flooding on multi-access segments by acting as a central relay

cards.front: Designated Router — reduces OSPF LSA flooding on multi-access segments by acting as a central relay

cards.back: Backup Designated Router — takes over instantly if the DR fails; promoted to DR, then a new BDR is elected

cards.front: Backup Designated Router — takes over instantly if the DR fails; promoted to DR, then a new BDR is elected
What two criteria decide OSPF DR election (in order)?
cards.back:
1. Highest OSPF interface priority (default 1, range 0–255)
2. Tie-breaker: highest Router ID (RID)

cards.front: Setting OSPF priority to [...] makes a router ineligible for DR/BDR.
On what OSPF network types is a DR/BDR elected?
cards.back: Broadcast and Non-Broadcast Multi-Access (NBMA). NOT on point-to-point or point-to-multipoint.
No — non-preemptive. A higher-priority router joining later does NOT take over; existing DR stays.

cards.front:
OSPF AllSPFRouters multicast address: [...]
OSPF AllDRouters multicast address: 224.0.0.6

OSPF AllSPFRouters multicast address: [...]
OSPF AllDRouters multicast address: 224.0.0.6
DROther routers send LSAs to which address?
cards.back: 224.0.0.6 (AllDRouters) — only DR and BDR listen. DR then re-floods to 224.0.0.5.
DR/BDR election on multi-access segment: routers exchange Hellos on 224.0.0.5 → compare priority (highest wins) → tie-breaker is highest RID → election is non-preemptive. Priority 0 = ineligible.
